claim.content: The coding process used open inductive coding whenever an existing taxonomy did not capture an observed rejection reason, resulting in the addition of four new rejection categories intended to capture agent-specific behaviors; one named category is "Submission for experimentation" (abbreviated "Experimentation"), defined as a PR created to experiment with or evaluate a coding agent or development tool and not intended for merging, which in the 654-PR sample was observed at 2.9% for Agentic-PRs and at 0.9% for Human-PRs; these newly introduced categories were included alongside the prior taxonomy to describe failure modes that were not present in earlier single-agent or human-focused taxonomies.

conclusion.content: The coding process used open inductive coding whenever an existing taxonomy did not capture an observed rejection reason, resulting in the addition of four new rejection categories intended to capture agent-specific behaviors; one named category is "Submission for experimentation" (abbreviated "Experimentation"), defined as a PR created to experiment with or evaluate a coding agent or development tool and not intended for merging, which in the 654-PR sample was observed at 2.9% for Agentic-PRs and at 0.9% for Human-PRs; these newly introduced categories were included alongside the prior taxonomy to describe failure modes that were not present in earlier single-agent or human-focused taxonomies.

premises.content: Define the coding process as: annotators first attempt to map each sampled PR to an existing rejection category from a prior taxonomy (Watanabe et al.); if no prior category adequately captures the observed reason, annotators perform inductive open coding to create a new category; newly created categories are then used as labels across the sample. The proposition is: "Categories introduced by inductive open coding under this procedure (for example, 'Submission for experimentation') are coherent constructs with clear inclusion criteria and are applied consistently across annotators such that their reported frequencies are reliable and comparable to prior taxonomy categories."

steps.reasoning:
Treat the stratified, annotated sample and coding process (conclusion 6) as established; note that coding used an initial taxonomy and allowed inductive addition of categories when comments did not fit the prior taxonomy.
[22]

Describe the methodological rule that produced new categories: the authors attempted to assign each sampled PR to the prior rejection categories and, when none fit, they applied open coding (inductive qualitative coding) to introduce new categories that capture behaviors observed in agentic PRs but not represented in the older taxonomy.

State the empirical result about new categories as reported: the authors added four new categories to the taxonomy to cover failure modes not present in earlier taxonomies; one explicitly named new category is "Submission for experimentation" (abbreviated Experimentation), which the coded data report at 2.9% for Agentic-PRs and 0.9% for Human-PRs.
Table 2

Provide the concrete example used to justify the "Submission for experimentation" category: the authors cite an experimentation-only PR in which the author wrote "Just an experiment to see how copilot would handle this," and the PR was closed, illustrating a PR opened for evaluation/testing of a coding agent rather than for integration.
[4]

Explain the paper's interpretive rationale: because autonomous agents frequently produce PRs for agent-evaluation, CI-triggering, or lifecycle actions not intended for merge, prior taxonomies that were human-focused could misclassify such cases; therefore augmenting the taxonomy with agent-focused categories enables faithful representation of agent-induced contribution types.
Table 2

motivating_questions.content: Existing PR-rejection taxonomies did not explicitly include several behaviors that are prevalent when autonomous agents generate PRs—examples include PRs intentionally opened for experimentation/evaluation of an agent, PRs serving merely to trigger CI for verification, and agent-driven lifecycle actions that are not aimed at actual integration. Because prior taxonomies lack these categories, analyses that reuse older codebooks risk misclassifying or overlooking agent-specific motivations for opening and closing PRs; extending the taxonomy is therefore necessary to faithfully represent the range of agent-induced contribution types observed in modern repositories.
